title: Dative — Verbs & Expressions
description: Practice dative-only verbs (pomáhat, rozumět, telefonovat, věřit) and dative expressions (líbit se, chutnat, je mi zima/teplo/dobře/špatně). Learn how these verbs and expressions require the person to be in dative case.

exercises.prompt: Which verb meaning 'to help' requires dative?

data.question_cz: Které sloveso vyžaduje dativ pro toho, komu se pomáhá?
data.question_en: Which verb requires dative for the person being helped?
data.explanation: 'Pomáhat' (to help) requires dative: Pomáhám bratrovi. = I help my brother. The person receiving help is in dative case.

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct dative: I help my mother.

data.explanation: 'Maminka' → dative 'mamince'. 'Pomáhat' always takes dative for the person being helped. Note the k→c consonant change: maminka → mamince.

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'I don't understand the professor.'

data.grammar_note: 'Rozumět' (to understand) takes dative for what you understand: rozumím profesorovi. Negation: nerozumím. Unlike English where 'understand' takes a direct object, Czech uses dative.

exercises.prompt: Which dative form is correct in this sentence?

data.explanation: 'Telefonovat' (to call/phone) takes dative for the person called: telefonuju kamarádce. 'Kamarádka' → dative 'kamarádce' with the k→c change.

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'Do you believe him?'

data.grammar_note: 'Věřit' (to believe) takes dative: věřit + dative. 'mu' is the dative short form of 'on' (he). Věříš = you believe (informal). Věříte = you believe (formal/plural).

exercises.prompt: Complete with the correct pronoun: They don't believe her.

data.explanation: 'Jí' is the dative form of 'ona' (she). Věřit + dative: věřím ti (I believe you), věřím mu (I believe him), věřím jí (I believe her). The short form 'jí' stays 'jí' in dative.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'I like Prague' using 'líbit se'?

data.question_en: How do you say 'I like Prague'?
data.explanation: 'Líbit se' (to like) uses dative for the person who likes: 'Líbí se mi Praha.' = literally 'Prague pleases me'. 'Mi' is the dative short form. The liked thing is the subject (Praha).

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'Do you like this book?'

data.grammar_note: 'Líbit se' + dative: 'líbí se ti' (you like, informal), 'líbí se vám' (you like, formal/plural). The book is the grammatical subject, so 'kniha' is nominative.

exercises.prompt: Complete: The soup tastes good (to me).

data.explanation: 'Chutnat' (to taste) works like 'líbit se': dative for who experiences the taste + nominative for the thing. 'Polévka mi chutná.' = The soup tastes good to me / I like the soup.

exercises.prompt: Which is the correct way to say 'I am cold' in Czech?

data.question_en: How do you say 'I am cold' in Czech?
data.explanation: 'Je mi zima.' = I am cold (literally 'it is cold to me'). Czech uses dative for sensations: je mi zima (I'm cold), je mi teplo (I'm warm), je mi dobře (I feel good), je mi špatně (I feel sick).

exercises.prompt: Complete: He feels sick. → Je ___ špatně.

data.explanation: 'Je mu špatně.' = He feels sick / nauseous. The dative pronoun carries the person: je mi (I), je ti (you), je mu (he), je jí (she), je nám (we), je vám (you pl.), je jim (they).

exercises.prompt: Translate to Czech: 'I feel good here.'

data.grammar_note: 'Je mi dobře.' = I feel good (dative construction). 'Tady/tu' = here. Sensations in Czech use dative + je: je mi zima/teplo/dobře/špatně/divně/příjemně.
